input_path: data\papers\Heithoff et al. - A Digital Shadow for Accurate Robot Motion Control Integrating Data with Friction Models.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The physical system under study is the first vertical robot axis of the MABI MAX 100 industrial robot, a six-axis serial robot used in manufacturing. This axis is selected for digital twinning due to its critical role in motion accuracy and the need to model friction dynamics for precise control.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The Digital Twin provides services for continuous, multi-physics-aware identification of joint friction in the robot's first vertical axis, enabling real-time monitoring and optimization of motion accuracy. These services support maintenance teams and control engineers by predicting wear and adjusting parameters to enhance operational precision.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: The Digital Twin implementation utilizes Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE) with SysML for system modeling across multiple abstraction levels (requirements, functional, logical, and technical layers). Architecture Description Languages (ADLs) such as ACME, AADL, MontiArc, and SysML Internal Block Diagrams (IBDs) model components, interfaces, interconnections, and composition. Behavior modeling is achieved through MontiArc Automaton and SysML v1.7 state machines, integrated with MathWorks Simulink for simulation, alongside general-purpose programming languages like C++ and Java for detailed behavior specification. These tools collectively address system complexity and integrate heterogeneous stakeholder perspectives.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: Computational models encompass structural components described via SysML Internal Block Diagrams (IBDs) and Architecture Description Languages (ADLs) such as ACME, AADL, and MontiArc, which define system components, interfaces, interconnections, and composition. Behavioral models utilize MontiArc Automaton, SysML state machines, and simulation tools like MathWorks Simulink for detailed dynamic behavior. Data sources originate from heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives, managed through integrated modeling approaches across varying abstraction levels (requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, product/technical layer) using multiple tools and notations.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: The physical acting components consist of a permanent magnet synchronous motor driving the first axis of the MABI MAX robot, coupled with a gear transmission system incorporating a cycloidal gear mechanism for precise torque transfer and motion control, with actuation ranges and safety constraints defined by mechanical design specifications.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Time series sensor data from physical assets is streamed via MQTT protocol as part of a one-way data flow, as stated: 'We have access to time series of all sensors, which are streamed via MQTT.'
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: MQTT protocol is used for streaming sensor data; no details on network topology, bandwidth, latency, redundancy, or security protocols are provided.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: The Digital Twin system adheres to industry standards including SysML for model-based systems engineering, ACME, AADL, and MontiArc for architecture description languages (ADLs), which specify component & connector perspectives, interfaces, interconnections, and composition to ensure interoperability across heterogeneous stakeholders and modeling tools.
extracted_characteristics.security_and_safety_considerations: The safety cell of the robot restricts the maximum working range to [-90°, 90°], and the maximum achievable speed is |q˙max| = 75°/s.

conclude in Sec. VII.


II. B ACKGROUND

To better understand our approach, we describe relevant
modeling methods, important concepts for DSs, and methods
on how to derive concrete DSs.


_A. Modeling Software and Systems_

To describe the structure and behavior of systems, ModelBased Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering
modeling languages such as SysML [17] during the development phase. Due to the complexity of systems, modeling
is typically split into different levels of abstraction, e.g.,
requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and
product/technical layer. There exist different methodologies
on how to handle this complexity, e.g., RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics) [18], [19], OOSEM [20],
SPES [21], or SMArDT [22]. To describe the functional
and logical architecture, typically architecture description languages (ADLs) are used. As main concepts, they describe
systems from a component & connector perspective, e.g.,
ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25], [26] or SysML
IBDs [17], [27]. These typically describe the system’s components, their interfaces, their interconnections, and composition.
Some ADLs also include methods to describe the system’s
behavior, e.g., MontiArc Automaton or state machines in
SysML v1.7. To describe behavior in more detail, these models
are often combined with further (modeling) tools such as
MathWorks Simulink [28], other simulation tools and methods [29], [30], or code in general-purpose languages like C++
or Java.

Thus, a comprehensive description of a system from an
MBSE perspective might have to cope with models in different
modeling notations and different tools. In addition, various
heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives need to be integrated [31].


_B. Digital Shadow Reference Model_

Kritzinger et al. [32] differentiate digital shadows from
digital twins based on their communication flow. A digital
shadow is characterized by “ _an automated one-way data_
_flow between the state of an existing physical object and_
_a digital object_ [32]”. In practical terms, this means that a
digital shadow functions as a passive entity: it neither operates
autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system.
Therefore, software systems require active components to
interpret and process this incoming data [33], [34]. A Digital
Twin (DT) can provide such active components [35]–[37].
In the remainder of this paper, we understand a digital
shadow as _“a set of contextual data traces and their ag-_
_gregation and abstraction collected for a specific purpose_



**Fig. 1:** Digital shadow reference model as a UML class
diagram. It defines important concepts for digital shadows in
manufacturing.

block_1_judge.reasoning: The extracted characteristic accurately describes the first vertical axis of the MABI MAX 100 six-axis serial robot, which is explicitly mentioned in the source as the focus for digital shadow creation. The source states: 'The focus of this paper is the serial robot MABI MAX 100 with six axes... The first vertical robot axis at the base is used for creating a digital shadow.' The characteristic correctly notes the axis's role in motion accuracy and friction modeling, which aligns with the source's emphasis on friction identification for accuracy improvement.

The extracted characteristic correctly identifies the digital shadow's purpose: 'continuous, multi-physics-aware identification of joint friction' and 'real-time monitoring and optimization of motion accuracy,' which aligns with the source: 'The resulting digital shadows enable the continuous, multi-physics-aware identification of joint friction... to enhance the robot’s motion accuracy.' However, specific claims about 'predicting wear' and 'adjusting parameters' for maintenance teams and control engineers are not explicitly supported in the provided source snippets, though they are plausible extensions.

The extracted text accurately and specifically details the tools and enablers mentioned in the source. The source text mentions: 'MBSE uses systems engineering modeling languages such as SysML' and that modeling is 'split into different levels of abstraction, e.g., requirements, functional, logical, and product/technical layer.' It also lists ADLs like ACME, AADL, MontiArc, and SysML IBDs, and behavior modeling with 'MontiArc Automaton or state machines in SysML v1.7.' It also mentions integration with MathWorks Simulink and general-purpose languages like C++ and Java. The extraction is a comprehensive and accurate summary.

The extraction is specific and largely supported, but includes a minor unsupported claim.
